Now, after going through all these details, I think the best answer is B: Generalized Myasthenia Gravis because the patient exhibits symptoms beyond just ptosis and diplopia, which are more typical of ocular MG. The presence of difficulty in chewing and swallowing suggests broader muscle involvement, consistent with generalized MG. Although anti-AChR antibodies were negative, it's important to remember that some patients may have seronegative forms of MG, especially those with variants like MuSK antibodies. Therefore, the clinical presentation along with the electrophysiologic findings strongly supports generalized MG as the most probable diagnosis.

Even though his AchR antibodies came back negative, we shouldn’t rule out MG entirely since some people don't produce detectable amounts of these antibodies.
